preceding: To solve this problem, we propose that users associate URIidentified Web resources from a knowledge context, such as Wikipedia, as part of their tagging activity.
target: A tagging system could suggest the label “Paris” as a possible tag in the user-interface, but create a link to a Web resource (e.g., http: //en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Paris) in the back-end.
trailing: We call this technique semantic tagging.

target: Semantic tagging, which is shown in Figure 1b, extends this model by representing a tag t as a qualified relationship between two resources: rx is the resource identifying and defining the semantics of a tag (e.g., http://en.wikipedia.org/ wiki/Paris), and ry is the resource being tagged (e.g., a photo taken in Paris).
trailing: The former is defined within a knowledge context K and can carry textual labels (e.g., “Paris”) and additional context information (e.g., Paris is a city in France).

preceding: However, URIs are opaque strings that do not necessarily carry any semantics.
target: While the design choice in Wikipedia was to use-human readable URIs (e.g., http:// en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Paris, other sources do not follow this approach.
trailing: In the GeoNames knowledge context, for instance, Paris is identified by a URI with a numeric path element http://www.geonames.org/2988507.
